title: Cat Constipation & Straining: Symptoms, Causes, and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your cat straining in the litter box? Learn about feline constipation, when it's an emergency, and how your vet can help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Feline constipation is generally a medium-urgency issue, but it becomes a life-threatening emergency if a cat is straining to urinate. If you are unsure whether your cat is struggling to poop or pee, contact a veterinarian immediately.

What are the common symptoms and signs of feline constipation?

  • Difficulty passing stool or defecating less frequently than usual.
  • Stool that is hard, dry, and difficult to expel.
  • Frequent trips to the litter box with straining.
  • Crying out or vocalizing while trying to go.
  • Leaving behind very small, hard nuggets of stool.

When is feline constipation considered a medical emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: Simple constipation is uncomfortable and can lead to permanent issues like megacolon if not treated, though it is rarely an instant crisis.
  • High Urgency: If a male cat is straining but not passing urine, this is a life-threatening urinary blockage.
  • Immediate Step: Contact a vet immediately if you cannot tell if your cat is straining to defecate or urinate.

How can taking a photo of my cat help the vet triage the situation?

  • A photo of your cat's posture in the litter box helps the vet assess the severity of the straining.
  • A photo of any stool produced allows the vet to evaluate consistency.
  • Visual evidence helps the vet differentiate between constipation, colitis, or a potential urinary blockage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Clinical signs of constipation include tenesmus and passage of firm, dry feces, which may appear thin or ribbon-like if the passage is hindered. Affected animals may exhibit lethargy, depression, anorexia, vomiting (especially in cats), and abdominal discomfort. Diagnosis is confirmed by a history of dietary indiscretion and physical evidence of retained feces. Abdominal palpation and rectal examination can confirm the presence of large volumes of retained fecal matter. Causes can include narrowed pelvic inlet (post-fracture), enlarged sublumbar lymph nodes or prostate gland, colonic stricture, megacolon (more common in cats), hypothyroidism, dysautonomia, spinal cord lesions, hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, and certain drugs (e.g., opioids, diuretics, antihistamines, anticholinergics, sucralfate, aluminum hydroxide, potassium bromide, calcium channel-blocking agents). Diagnostic tests such as abdominal radiographs, barium enema, ultrasonography, or colonoscopy may be needed to identify obstructive lesions. A CBC, biochemical profile including a serum T4 level, urinalysis, and detailed neurologic examination should be completed in cases of chronic or recurring constipation. Mild constipation can often be treated by dietary adjustment consisting of avoidance of dietary indiscretion, ready access to water and high-fiber diets, and use of suppository laxatives. Long-term use of laxatives should be discouraged unless absolutely necessary.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: How often should my cat have a bowel movement?
faqs.answer: Most healthy cats will defecate at least once every 24 to 36 hours. If your cat hasn't gone in over 48 hours, you should consult your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat a laxative at home?
faqs.answer: You should never give your cat human laxatives or home remedies like mineral oil without professional guidance, as these can cause aspiration or dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

faqs.question: What are common causes of constipation in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dehydration, ingestion of hairballs, lack of fiber in the diet, or underlying medical conditions like kidney disease or arthritis that makes posing difficult.
